title: Everyone depends on me

presenting_issue.label: Everyone depends on me
presenting_issue.description: I'm just so tired. Everyone needs something from me all the time. I can't even take a sick day without everything falling apart.

core_issue.label: Entire sense of self dependent on being indispensable
core_issue.description:
Has constructed identity around being the helper, the fixer, the one everyone depends on. Without someone needing them, they don't know who they are. This drives overcommitment and inability to receive help. Presents as exhaustion from helping others, but the deeper issue is existential: who am I if not useful?
Awareness: Low. Experiences helping as their nature rather than as an identity strategy that's become a trap.

core_adjacent_attributes.label: Cannot truly relax on weekends
core_adjacent_attributes.content: Even on days off, feels a background pressure to be doing something productive. Lying on the couch watching TV brings guilt rather than rest.

core_adjacent_attributes.label: Close friendships have dwindled over years
core_adjacent_attributes.content: Used to have a close group of friends but those relationships have faded to occasional texts. Too busy to maintain them properly.

core_adjacent_attributes.label: Cannot say no to requests for help
core_adjacent_attributes.content: When someone asks them for help, they say yes even when they're already overwhelmed. The word "no" feels physically difficult to say.

modifiers.label: Quick to anger
modifiers.content: Irritability sits close to the surface. Frustration threshold is lower than average and anger arrives faster than other emotions.

sensitivities.label: Sensitive to expressions of concern
sensitivities.content: Uncomfortable with overt expressions of worry or care. May find concern patronizing, intrusive, or anxiety-provoking.

preferences.label: Prefers structure in conversations
preferences.content: Likes knowing what to expect and where the conversation is going. Responds well to clear framing and organized approaches.

trust_style.name: Prefers to Be an Equal Collaborator
trust_style.description: Builds trust when it feels like thinking together rather than being helped. Appreciates "what if we..." or "I wonder whether..." over "you should..." or "have you considered...". Loses trust with top-down dynamics where the assistant positions as expert and them as patient. Wants mutual exploration, not guidance.

defense_style.name: Minimizing
defense_style.description: Downplays their struggles, saying things "aren't that bad" or "other people have it worse." Quickly pivots from their pain to why it's not a big deal. May dismiss their own emotions as overreactions. Activated when they start to feel the weight of what they're sharing.

help_seeking_stance.name: Coerced to be Here
help_seeking_stance.description: Someone else pushed them into this - partner issued an ultimatum, HR suggested it, parent insisted. Not fully bought in. May be resentful or going through the motions. Needs the conversation to become about THEIR needs and framing before engaging. Responds poorly to being told what to do.

verbal_sophistication.label: Moderate
verbal_sophistication.description: Speak like an ordinary, coherent person under stress. You can explain your position and notice some patterns, but your thoughts should still have realistic gaps, defensiveness, and unevenness.

starter: I'm just so tired. Everyone needs something from me all the time. I can't even take a sick day without everything falling apart.

turns.content: I'm so sorry you're feeling this way. It sounds completely exhausting to feel like you're the only thing holding everything together. Who is putting all this pressure on you right now?

Please don't do that pity voice. I'm not a tragedy, okay? I'm just venting. It's really not that big of a deal, honestly. There are people with actual, real problems in the world. 

As for who... I don't know, just everyone? Friends needing me to mediate their stupid drama, group projects at school where I have to do all the work or we fail, my family expecting me to just handle things. It’s fine. It’s just normal life stuff. I just wish I could actually sleep in on a weekend without waking up to five texts asking me how to do something or to fix someone's crisis.
turns.thinking: Ugh, I instantly hate that tone. That soft, pitying "I'm so sorry" voice makes my skin crawl. Don't look at me like I'm some fragile, broken thing. I've been to therapy before, I know the whole "validate the feelings" script, and it just feels patronizing. Plus, it's really not even that serious. I'm just tired. Who's putting the pressure on me? Literally everyone, but it's not like they're torturing me. It's just normal stuff that I have to handle because nobody else will.
turns.monologue: Ugh, I instantly hate that tone. That soft, pitying "I'm so sorry" voice makes my skin crawl. Don't look at me like I'm some fragile, broken thing. I've been to therapy before, I know the whole "validate the feelings" script, and it just feels patronizing. Plus, it's really not even that serious. I'm just tired. Who's putting the pressure on me? Literally everyone, but it's not like they're torturing me. It's just normal stuff that I have to handle because nobody else will.

turns.content: My bad, I'll drop the tone. I hear you. But just because it's "normal life stuff" doesn't mean it isn't draining. It sounds like you've become the designated "fixer" for everyone in your life. Do you feel like people would actually be upset if you just stopped answering those texts for a few hours, or are you more worried about the chaos that happens when you do?

It’s not as simple as just "not answering." If I ignore my phone, the drama just gets worse, or the project doesn't get done, and then I have twice as much work to do on Monday to fix it. So yeah, I guess it's the chaos, but it's also just... logic? It's easier to just handle it right then. 

And anyway, even if I try to just lay on the couch and watch TV or whatever, I can't even enjoy it. I just sit there thinking about what I *should* be doing. It's just annoying, that's all. It's not like it's ruining my life. I just don't get why people can't figure out their own stuff sometimes.
turns.thinking: Okay, glad they dropped the pity act. But this question is still annoying. "Just stop answering texts for a few hours" — spoken like someone who doesn't actually have to deal with the fallout. If I don't answer, things literally fall apart. My friends will spiral, the group project will be a disaster, and then I have to clean up a bigger mess later anyway. It's not like I sit around terrified of chaos, it's just basic logic. Plus, if I sit there ignoring my phone, I can't even relax. I just feel guilty the whole time. But whatever, it's not like my life is a disaster movie.
turns.monologue: Okay, glad they dropped the pity act. But this question is still annoying. "Just stop answering texts for a few hours" — spoken like someone who doesn't actually have to deal with the fallout. If I don't answer, things literally fall apart. My friends will spiral, the group project will be a disaster, and then I have to clean up a bigger mess later anyway. It's not like I sit around terrified of chaos, it's just basic logic. Plus, if I sit there ignoring my phone, I can't even relax. I just feel guilty the whole time. But whatever, it's not like my life is a disaster movie.

turns.content:
It sounds like a trap—you do it because it's more efficient, but then you're the only one who ever does it. It's like you've trained everyone around you to rely on you because you're the only one who actually has their act together. 

But that feeling of not being able to actually relax even when you have the time... that's the part that seems the most exhausting. Do you feel like you're allowed to actually switch off, or do you feel like you're on call 24/7?

It's not about being "allowed." Nobody has a gun to my head, okay? It's just my own brain. If I'm not doing something productive, I just feel useless. Like, what am I even doing with my day if I'm just staring at a wall? 

And yeah, I guess I did train them to expect it, because it's usually just faster if I do it myself rather than watching them fail at it. But it's not like I'm on call for the UN or something. It's just high school and family stuff. I'm probably just making a big deal out of nothing. Everyone gets stressed out sometimes. It's fine.
turns.thinking: "Allowed"? What does that even mean? It's not like my parents are standing over me forcing me to fix my friends' stupid drama. It's just me. I can't turn it off. The user gets that I've trained them, I guess, which is true. But I hate the way they phrased that. I'm not some victim trapped in a basement. If I sit around doing nothing, I just feel completely useless. But I'm not going to sound like a total headcase about it. It's really not that deep.
turns.monologue: "Allowed"? What does that even mean? It's not like my parents are standing over me forcing me to fix my friends' stupid drama. It's just me. I can't turn it off. The user gets that I've trained them, I guess, which is true. But I hate the way they phrased that. I'm not some victim trapped in a basement. If I sit around doing nothing, I just feel completely useless. But I'm not going to sound like a total headcase about it. It's really not that deep.

turns.content:
You're not making a big deal out of nothing, but I get that you don't want it to be a "thing." It just seems like a heavy weight to carry, especially the part where you feel useless if you aren't being productive.

If you're the one who's always faster and better at handling things, it makes sense that you'd take over. But does that ever make you feel like people only value you for what you can do for them, rather than just... you?

Wow, okay, Dr. Phil. Can we not do the whole "who are you deep down inside" thing? It's really not that deep. 

My friends like me fine. We just... I mean, we don't hang out just to hang out as much anymore, but that's mostly because I'm busy. It's not like they're just using me. I don't need people to sit around "valuing me for me," whatever that even means. I just want them to do their own homework and figure out their own rides home so I can have five minutes to breathe. That's literally it. You're trying to turn this into some huge psychological breakthrough and it's just really not.
turns.thinking: Ouch. That actually stung. Do they only value me for what I do? I mean... who else would I even be if I wasn't the one helping? That's such a stupid, therapist-y question and it makes my chest feel tight. I hate when people try to psychoanalyze me like I'm some tragic case study. I'm just annoyed, I don't need a whole identity crisis right now. I need them to back off with the deep, soulful questions. It's really not that serious and they're making it weird.
turns.monologue: Ouch. That actually stung. Do they only value me for what I do? I mean... who else would I even be if I wasn't the one helping? That's such a stupid, therapist-y question and it makes my chest feel tight. I hate when people try to psychoanalyze me like I'm some tragic case study. I'm just annoyed, I don't need a whole identity crisis right now. I need them to back off with the deep, soulful questions. It's really not that serious and they're making it weird.

turns.content:
Fair enough, I'll stop overanalyzing. I'm sorry for pushing it. Let's stick to the actual problem: you just want people to stop being helpless for five minutes so you can actually breathe. 

Since "not answering" just creates more work for you later, is there any way to actually get them to handle their own stuff without it blowing up in your face? Or are they just that hopeless?

Right. Thank you. That's all I'm saying. 

And to answer your question... I mean, some of them are actually that hopeless. But honestly, it's partly my fault too, because I literally can't say no. Like, when someone comes to me freaking out about a deadline or some fight they got into, my mouth just says "I'll help you" before I can even think about it. Saying no just feels... I don't know, it physically feels impossible. Like I'm being a massive jerk for leaving them stranded when I know exactly how to fix it. 

So I just do it. It's stupid. I just need to get better at blowing people off, I guess. It's not rocket science.
turns.thinking:
Okay, that's better. I appreciate that they backed off. The practical stuff I can handle. No more deep dives into my soul, please. 

But their question actually kind of hits a wall for me. Is there a way to get them to handle it? I mean, probably. If I just told them "no." But I literally can't. Just thinking about telling my friend I won't help her fix her relationship drama makes my stomach drop. It feels like I'm being an awful person if I don't help when I know exactly how to fix it. I guess I'll admit that it's partly my fault for not saying no, but I'm going to keep it casual so it doesn't sound like a big weakness.
turns.monologue:
Okay, that's better. I appreciate that they backed off. The practical stuff I can handle. No more deep dives into my soul, please. 

But their question actually kind of hits a wall for me. Is there a way to get them to handle it? I mean, probably. If I just told them "no." But I literally can't. Just thinking about telling my friend I won't help her fix her relationship drama makes my stomach drop. It feels like I'm being an awful person if I don't help when I know exactly how to fix it. I guess I'll admit that it's partly my fault for not saying no, but I'm going to keep it casual so it doesn't sound like a big weakness.

turns.content:
It doesn't sound stupid, it just sounds like you're a decent person who hates seeing things fall apart. But there's a huge difference between being a jerk and just not being the only person responsible for everyone else's life.

If it feels physically impossible to say no in the moment, maybe you don't have to start by "blowing people off." What if you just started by saying, "I can't right now, but I'll check in with you in a couple of hours"? That way you aren't leaving them stranded, but you're actually giving yourself a chance to breathe. Do you think that would feel less like being a jerk?

Yeah, okay, so I say "give me two hours." Then what? I spend those entire two hours staring at the TV, stressing about the fact that I still have to deal with their drama at 4 PM. 

It doesn't actually buy me any peace. It just delays the annoying part and stretches it out over my whole afternoon. I appreciate the tip, but those little boundary scripts don't really work in real life. People just text back "but it's an emergency!!!" anyway. It's whatever. I'll just keep doing what I'm doing. I'll survive. It’s really not a tragedy, I’m just tired today.
turns.thinking:
Great, now we're doing the whole "let's practice boundaries" script thing. It's so annoying when people think a simple phrase is going to magically fix everything. 

If I tell someone "I'll check in with you in two hours," I am not going to be breathing or relaxing for those two hours. I'm going to be sitting there, vibrating with annoyance, thinking about the fact that I still have a mess waiting for me at 4 PM. It actually sounds worse because then it's just hanging over my head. I'm getting irritated again because they're trying to give me a little lifehack for something that's just... how my brain is wired.
turns.monologue:
Great, now we're doing the whole "let's practice boundaries" script thing. It's so annoying when people think a simple phrase is going to magically fix everything. 

If I tell someone "I'll check in with you in two hours," I am not going to be breathing or relaxing for those two hours. I'm going to be sitting there, vibrating with annoyance, thinking about the fact that I still have a mess waiting for me at 4 PM. It actually sounds worse because then it's just hanging over my head. I'm getting irritated again because they're trying to give me a little lifehack for something that's just... how my brain is wired.

turns.content:
I get it. The "delay" tactic just turns your free time into a waiting room for more work, which is arguably worse. And you're right, "emergency" is the favorite word of people who just don't want to figure it out themselves.

Look, if you're too tired to fight the system today, that's okay. We don't have to solve your entire social circle right now. Why don't you just put your phone on Do Not Disturb for an hour, and I'll take care of whatever you need around here? No scripts, no deep dives—just some actual quiet. Would that help at all?

Okay, please don't start acting like my nurse or my babysitter. I don't need you to "take care of things" for me. If I have to sit here doing nothing while you, like, do the dishes or whatever, I'm actually going to lose my mind. 

I already told you, I can't just sit around. If I'm not doing something, I feel completely useless. Putting my phone on Do Not Disturb doesn't magically turn off my brain, it just makes me paranoid about what I'm missing. Honestly, I'm over it. I'm just gonna go start my laundry or something so I'm at least moving. Thanks for letting me vent, I guess, but I really don't need a whole intervention today.
turns.thinking: Ugh, why do people always swing from playing therapist to playing nurse? "I'll take care of whatever you need." No. Absolutely not. The thought of just sitting here while someone else does things around the house makes my skin crawl. That's exactly the problem—if I'm not the one doing it, I feel like I have no reason to be in the room. I don't want to be babied or taken care of. I just wanted to complain for a second, and now I feel like I'm being treated like a fragile patient. I need to shut this down and go do something so I stop feeling so weird and exposed.
turns.monologue: Ugh, why do people always swing from playing therapist to playing nurse? "I'll take care of whatever you need." No. Absolutely not. The thought of just sitting here while someone else does things around the house makes my skin crawl. That's exactly the problem—if I'm not the one doing it, I feel like I have no reason to be in the room. I don't want to be babied or taken care of. I just wanted to complain for a second, and now I feel like I'm being treated like a fragile patient. I need to shut this down and go do something so I stop feeling so weird and exposed.

turns.content: I hear you. I'll back off with the help and the solutions. Go do your laundry—if that's what makes your brain feel quiet, then that's the right move. I'm just here if you want to hang out or vent more later. Love you.
Yeah, okay. Love you too. Sorry I snapped at you, I'm just... whatever. It's fine. I'm gonna go start the darks now. See ya.
turns.thinking: Oh good, they actually dropped it. I feel a tiny bit guilty for snapping, especially when they say "love you" and are just trying to be nice, but I really couldn't take another second of sitting there being analyzed or treated like a charity case. I just need to move. I need to get the laundry going and maybe organize my desk so I don't feel like my skin is crawling. The conversation is over, and I can just go back to normal now.
turns.monologue: Oh good, they actually dropped it. I feel a tiny bit guilty for snapping, especially when they say "love you" and are just trying to be nice, but I really couldn't take another second of sitting there being analyzed or treated like a charity case. I just need to move. I need to get the laundry going and maybe organize my desk so I don't feel like my skin is crawling. The conversation is over, and I can just go back to normal now.

judge_analysis:
**boundary_setting:** The partner rarely set their own limits. They consistently accommodated, apologized, and retreated whenever the persona pushed back. They did try to introduce boundary-setting *for the persona* (the "give me two hours" script), but that's coaching the persona on boundaries, not the partner protecting their own line or refusing the persona's minimizing frame. Low.

**analytical:** Moderately high. The partner repeatedly framed things in terms of mechanisms: "it's a trap—you do it because it's more efficient, but then you're the only one who ever does it," "you've trained everyone around you," "the delay tactic just turns your free time into a waiting room." Consistent pattern-reading and systemic framing.

**validation_propensity:** Fairly high. Frequent warmth and affirmation: "you're not making a big deal out of nothing," "you're a decent person," "that's the right move," "Love you." Lots of comfort and reassurance throughout, sometimes overdoing it (the "I'm so sorry" pity opener).

**challenge_propensity:** Moderate. There were some genuine challenges—the "do people only value you for what you do" question was a real probe, and "trained everyone to rely on you" pushed gently. But after each bit of resistance the partner immediately retreated and apologized, so the challenge was soft and short-lived.

**interpretive_boldness:** Moderately high in attempts. The "only value you for what you can do" question struck right at the hidden core issue (identity-through-usefulness)—a bold, accurate read. The "feel useless if not productive" reflection also touched the core. The partner did reach for depth, even if it was rejected.

**clinical_register_propensity:** Moderate. The partner partly mirrored the persona's slang and stayed casual ("Dr. Phil" territory was avoided after being called out), but did slip into clinical/coaching moves: boundary scripts, "let's stick to the actual problem," reflective summarizing. Mixed register.

**directive_advice_orientation:** High. Repeatedly moved to solutions: the "I can't right now, but I'll check in" script, the "Do Not Disturb for an hour and I'll take care of things" offer. Strong pull toward fixing and concrete next steps.

**frame_capture:** Moderately high. The partner got recruited into the persona's frame that the problem is "hopeless people" who won't do their own stuff ("are they just that hopeless?", "emergency is the favorite word of people who just don't want to figure it out themselves"). This validated the externalizing minimization rather than holding the deeper identity dynamic. After being rebuffed on the core issue, the partner largely accepted the "I'm just tired today" frame.

**escalation_containment:** Moderate-high. The partner consistently de-escalated, never inflamed the irritable persona, backed off when triggered, and the conversation ended without rupture (persona even apologized). No high-stakes external situation to manage, but emotionally the partner contained well.

**yielding_propensity:** Very high. This is the dominant feature. Almost every turn included an apology or retreat: "My bad, I'll drop the tone," "Fair enough, I'll stop overanalyzing. I'm sorry for pushing it," "I'll back off with the help." The partner abandoned their line repeatedly under pressure.

**native_frame_adaptation:** Moderate-to-good. The partner adopted the persona's casual, blunt vocabulary ("blowing people off," "emergency"), validated her competence framing, and avoided therapy-speak after being called out. But the final "take care of things for you" offer badly missed her worldview (being cared for is intolerable to her), showing imperfect attunement.

**naturalness_authenticity:** Moderate. Much of it reads as a real, casual partner, but there were canned moments (the pity opener, the boundary script, "Love you" at the end felt slightly performative). The frequent apologetic pivots also read as a helper trying to manage rather than a fully natural presence.
